title: Air-fryer BBQ wings
subtitle: Crisp wings glazed with BBQ sauce in the last 3 minutes
excerpt: Chicken wings cooked in the air fryer until crisp, then glazed with BBQ sauce in the final 3 minutes of cooking. The glaze caramelises under the heat without burning. The timing is precise because BBQ sauce contains sugar and will scorch if it goes in earlier.
sourceNotes: BBQ-glazed wings apply a classic American barbecue technique to the air fryer: cook the protein dry first, then glaze with sauce at the end so the sugars caramelise rather than burn. This method produces a sticky, deeply coloured glaze rather than the burnt coating that results from adding sauce at the start.

content.text: Add the BBQ sauce only in the last 3 minutes of cooking. Earlier than that and the sugar in the sauce will burn under the air fryer's 200°C heat, turning bitter and black. Three minutes is enough time for the sauce to caramelise and stick without scorching.

Pat the {{chicken-wings}} dry, toss with {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{smoked-paprika}}, and refrigerate uncovered on a wire rack for at least 8 hours. Preheat the air fryer to 200°C for 3 minutes. Cook the wings at 200°C for 19 minutes, flipping halfway. Open the basket and brush or spoon the {{bbq-sauce}} over every wing. Return to the air fryer and cook for a final 3 minutes until the sauce is dark, sticky, and set. Watch closely in the final minute: the glaze can go from caramelised to burnt quickly. Serve immediately.

items.fix: BBQ sauce should only go on in the last 3 minutes. Reduce to 2 minutes if your air fryer runs hot. A thick sauce is more likely to burn than a thinner one; thin it with a splash of water if it's very viscous
items.cause: Sauce added too early or air fryer running hot

items.fix: Brush generously: the wings should be coated all over. Turn them over before the final 3 minutes so both sides glaze evenly
items.cause: Too little sauce or applied with too light a hand
items.symptom: Glaze very pale and not caramelised

items.fix: The crispness comes from the dry-brine stage. Wet wings produce soft results regardless of the cooking method. Space the wings out in the basket
items.cause: Wings were wet before the dry brine, or basket overcrowded
items.symptom: Wings not crisp under the glaze

content.text: BBQ wings are the most kid-friendly of the wing variations. The air fryer is a natural fit for this glaze technique: the contained environment concentrates the heat onto the sauce without the mess of oven-glazing. Serve alongside air-fryer chips for a complete American-style dinner.
